data.theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
data.title: John Nava Oral History Video
data.description: John Nava discusses growing up in Pueblo, Colorado on "Goat Hill" in the 1940's and 1950's. This interview covers his time in the Navy, his educational journey, career, and his experiences with discrimination. Mr. Nava was an educator for over fifty years. His past experiences helped him decide what kind of teacher he would become to best support his students. He taught several subjects including Chicano Studies and was instrumental in founding the Central High School Spanish Club and the Student Exchange Program to Hermosillo, Mexico.

value.text: University of Washington Minority Affairs Office
University of Washington Minority Affairs Office

data.theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
data.title: Becky Hobbs at Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento, Seattle, August 22, 1974
data.description: In this image of Rebecca "Becky" Hobbs, the University of Washington Minority Affairs Office staff assistant discusses the first annual Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento (Women in the Movement conference), held over three days at El Centro de La Raza on Beacon Hill. The event was an initial step in establishing a state-wide network for job listings, education opportunities, legislative lobbying, and support. Ms. Hobbs was quoted as saying, "Employers don't seem to think there are any Chicanas in Seattle. We know others are out there and don't want to lose them. We want to keep Chicana talent here. But first we must provide some job-skill training, a log of information on day care, resume writing workshops - whatever is needed to get the Chicana out of the background." Another hope for the conference was to bridge the gap between Chicanas in Eastern and Western Washington. Amalia Garza, an attendee, said, "Since we are so different, we can tap that many more talents. If we solve the practical problems, we can deal with the philosophical." The event was hosted by the Chicano Division and MECHA at the University of Washington, and funded by the Chicano Mobile Institute through the University of Utah. Caption information source: "Chicanas: 'Now It's Our Turn,'" Seattle Post-Intelligence, August 23, 1974, page 44.

data.theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
data.title: Oral history of Charlie J. Jaquez Jr.
data.description: Description of content: Jaquez talks about his activism in the Chicano movement. , Physical description: 1 digital file in transcript form only. , Project Description: As part of the advisory committee for the exhibit El Movimiento, Daniel Salazar interviewed Carlos Santistevan on August 13, 2014. This oral history was the genesis for the 2018-2019 Movimiento Elders Project. The Movimiento Elders Project, per Salazar, was open to all interested in participating. The interviews began by asking each participant "When did you become Chicano?" with discussion following aimed to document the history of the Chicano Movement in Colorado. Organized by Salazar, himself a Colorado native and descendent of Hispanic and Mexican immigrants, the project included Salazar photographing each participant and transcribing each interview. The Movimiento Elders Project was an independent documentation project, conducted by Salazar and donated to History Colorado in 2021.

data.theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
data.title: Civil Rights Movement scrapbooks. Mississippi events volume 1
data.description: Topics covered: segregation "watchdog" committee; Parker lynching; "Freedom Riders"; Philadelphia black church burned; voting registration disputes; James H. Meredith; contempt of court charges sought against University of Mississippi officials; Governor Ross Barnett bars Meredith from "Ole Miss". These scrapbooks, compiled by librarians at the Birmingham Public Library, contain newspaper clippings relating to the Civil Rights Movement. The clippings are arranged in three subject areas: national civil rights events, Alabama events, and Mississippi events. The clippings are arranged chronologically within the subject areas.

data.theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
data.title: Catholicism and the Civil Rights Movement
data.description: Encyclopedia article about the Catholic Church's involvement with the civil rights movement in Alabama. Most of Alabama's white Catholics shared white southerners' racism and initially opposed the goals of the movement. They preferred order and stability instead of activism for integration and racial justice. Catholic teaching clearly opposed racial discrimination, however, and after the mid-1960s there was little sympathy for segregation. For most of the civil rights movement, the Catholic Church in Alabama remained on the margins of the debates over integration and focused on internal Church affairs. It took the 1965 Selma to Montgomery march for voting rights to draw the Church from the margins into the mainstream of the movement. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata.

data.theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
data.title: [Floyd Arnold "Jim" Crow and Football Team]
data.description: Photograph of Floyd Arnold "Jim" Crow and a football team. The men in the first row, sitting cross legged, are, from left to right, unidentified, Ben Dean Sheats, Jack Southern, Garland Fawcett, and a man known as "Cheetah". The men in the second row are marked as Cook, Baughman, Curtis, La Grone, Marrs McLean, and Barney Hamm. Those in the last row are Jim Crow, a man known as "Deacon", Vasey, Goolachek, Mouser, Kazanas, and Lyday. Crow wears a jacket with the letter "B" on it, and Jack Southern holds a football in his lap. 1 photograph : b&w ; 21 x 26 cm.

data.theme: "Stonewall riots" OR "Stonewall uprising" OR "Stonewall Rebellion"
data.title: American Experience; Interview with Martha Shelley, 2 of 2 Stonewall Uprising
data.description: In this interview, Martha Shelley discusses repression in the 1950's, the Shelley Radio Show at Barnard College, her experience in the Daughters of Bilitis, the Mattachine Society, police brutality, bar raids, and organizing the Gay Liberation Front after the Stonewall uprising. In the early morning hours of June 28, 1969 police raided the Stonewall Inn, a popular gay bar in the Greenwich Village section of New York City. Such raids were not unusual in the late 1960s, an era when homosexual sex was illegal in every state but Illinois. That night, however, the street erupted into violent protests and street demonstrations that lasted for the next six days. The Stonewall riots, as they came to be known, marked a major turning point in the modern gay civil rights movement in the United States and around the world.

data.theme: "Queer oral history" OR "LGBTQ oral history" OR "Gay oral history"
data.title: Audrey, Oral History Interview, 2024
data.description: In this interview a genderfluid community member named Audrey shares their experiences with Ypsi Pride, explaining that they were grateful to be there. Audrey explores their history with activism, detailing their involvement with the encampment for Palestine on U of M's diag, the work they do preserving queer history, and their radio show. They express that they want others to have compassion for themselves and those around them, and that the future is worth fighting for.

data.theme: "Queer oral history" OR "LGBTQ oral history" OR "Gay oral history"
data.title: Jere Mitchum tells about being discharged from the army for being gay, oral history excerpt, 2011 January 10
data.description: Excerpt from an oral history interview with Jeremiah "Jere" Mitchum, conducted on January 10, 2011 by Phil Bell as part of the Brooks Fund History Project. Mitchum discusses joining the Army and receiving a general discharge after basic training for acknowledging that he was homosexual on his intake forms. Mitchum jokes that he tells people he fought in the "battle of Fort Benning, Georgia" and laughs about his experience. The complete interview, as well as a transcript and supplemental materials, are available in the Special Collections Division. Clip located on file BFHPMitchumJ from timecode 02:50:51 - 02:52:54. Transcript location, pages 94-95. Forms part of the Brooks Fund History Project.

data.theme: "Queer oral history" OR "LGBTQ oral history" OR "Gay oral history"
data.title: Anonymous, Oral History Interview, 2023
data.description: In this interview an anonymous parent and their child share their experiences with Ypsi Pride. Anon shares how they have been coming to Ypsi Pride for many years, and explains that while their child, who is autistic, does not normally like big gatherings they love pride events. Anon also explains how they did not meet another queer person until they were an adult so they want to expose their child to the beauty that exists in the queer community.

data.description: In this interview, Virginia Apuzzo talks about reconciling faith and sexuality; LGBTQ oppression from political, religious, and medical institutions; the civil rights movement; and the role of bars for the gay community in New York. In the early morning hours of June 28, 1969 police raided the Stonewall Inn, a popular gay bar in the Greenwich Village section of New York City. Such raids were not unusual in the late 1960s, an era when homosexual sex was illegal in every state but Illinois. That night, however, the street erupted into violent protests and street demonstrations that lasted for the next six days. The Stonewall riots, as they came to be known, marked a major turning point in the modern gay civil rights movement in the United States and around the world.

data.description: Interviews conducted by Emma Cieslik, senior Biology and History major at Ball State University, for the Muncie LGBTQ+ Oral History Project. Cieslik began working with the project as a student in Dr. Emily Johnson's HIST303 Oral History Workshop, then continued as part of an honors undergraduate research fellowship through Ball State's Honors College. Roddy's camera turns off for approximately nine minutes around the 01:09:00 mark. This archival material has been provided for educational purposes. Ball State University Libraries recognizes that some historic items may include offensive content. Our statement regarding objectionable content is available at: https://dmr.bsu.edu/digital/about

data.theme: "Stonewall riots" OR "Stonewall uprising" OR "Stonewall Rebellion"
data.title: American Experience; Interview with Martin Boyce, 1 of 4 Stonewall Uprising
data.description: In this interview, Martin Boyce discusses homophobia and oppression in the 1950's and 1960's, as well as LGBTQ culture at the time. Topics include early gay cultural icons and literary figures; cruising, drag, and bar culture in Greenwich Village; and police brutality and bar raids. Boyce also discusses his personal experience at Stonewall and the impact and legacy of the uprising. In the early morning hours of June 28, 1969 police raided the Stonewall Inn, a popular gay bar in the Greenwich Village section of New York City. Such raids were not unusual in the late 1960s, an era when homosexual sex was illegal in every state but Illinois. That night, however, the street erupted into violent protests and street demonstrations that lasted for the next six days. The Stonewall riots, as they came to be known, marked a major turning point in the modern gay civil rights movement in the United States and around the world.

data.theme: "Queer oral history" OR "LGBTQ oral history" OR "Gay oral history"
data.title: Cincinnati LGBTQ+ oral history interviews: Scott Knox
data.description: CHPL's Genealogy and Local History department is honoring the 50-year anniversary of the first Cincinnati Pride, which took place in 1973, through digital artifacts and oral history interviews with notable and elder members of the Cincinnati and Northern Kentucky LGBTQIA+ community. Scott Knox (he/him) has a Master’s degree from the University of Cincinnati Medical College and a law degree from the University of Cincinnati College of Law. He practices law in his private practice, Scott E. Knox Attorneys at Law, and specializes in LGBTQ+ legal issues. He sits on several Greater Cincinnati nonprofit boards including Strategies to End Homelessness, Equality Cincinnati, the Ohio Lesbian Archives, and the Cincinnati and Hamilton County Library Foundation. He has won numerous awards for his LGBTQ+ and community legal advocacy, argued successfully at the Ohio Supreme Court, and was named Cincinnati CityBeat magazine’s Best Lawyer for 2011, 2012, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2018, and 2019. Scott was the Grand Marshal for Cincinnati Pride in 2005. *This video contains adult language and content.

data.theme: "Trail of Tears" OR "Indian Removal Act"
data.title: 1838 Apr. 11, Washington City [to] N[ewton] Cannon, Tennessee / Winfield Scott
data.description: This is a letter from Winfield Scott, the Federal officer in charge of Cherokee Removal. It is to Governor Newton Cannon, dated April 11, 1838. Scott is asking Cannon to send him troops for his army. He details the positions he needs filled.

data.theme: "Trail of Tears" OR "Indian Removal Act"
data.title: Notice, 1838 Jan. 20, [to] the Cherokees / Nat[haniel] Smith
data.description: This is a notice from General Nathaniel Smith, Superintendent of Cherokee emigration, to the Cherokee people, dated January 20, 1838. Smith informs the Cherokees that steam boats will be available for their transportation to a territory west of the Mississippi River, but emigrants may choose the overland route as well. He reminds the Cherokees of the sentiments of the President on the removal issue and warns them to remove early or face military force.

data.theme: "Red Power movement" OR "Red Power" OR "American Indian Movement"
data.title: On the Formation of the American Indian Movement; N.D
data.description: Both sides: How and why the American Indian Movement (AIM) was formed in Minneapolis, MN. First speaker talks about the issues within his community which prompted the creation of an organization which would benefit them, unlike the US government and/or christian organizations that donâ€šÃ„Ã ́t have their best interest in mind. Created and adult education program to help minimize further exploitation by the traders who come to the reservation, their legal rights, and so on. Education to preserve their culture. Discussion is recorded in a conference type setting and some of the audio is low or inaudible. Digitization made possible through a Recordings at Risk grant from the Council of Libraries and Information Resources.

data.theme: "Trail of Tears" OR "Indian Removal Act"
data.title: Recounting the Removal: Recent Native American Literary Reconstructions of the Trail of Tears
data.description: Article provides a literary analysis of four novels written by native writers about the Trail of Tears. Frederick Hale explores the ways these reconstructions elaborate on a period of time and a perspective often marginalized in U.S. historical narrative.

value.text: Institute of Museum and Library Services
Institute of Museum and Library Services

data.theme: "Trail of Tears" OR "Indian Removal Act"
data.title: [Letter] 1840 June 18, Conasauga Plains [to] W[i]l[lia]m H. Thomas, Washington D.C. / Nicholas S. Peck
data.description: This is a letter, dated June 18, 1840, from Nicholas S. Peck to William H. Thomas, legal representative for the Eastern Cherokees of North Carolina. Peck states that each head of an Indian family received 640 acres of land under the treaty of 1817, but when Andrew Jackson became President, those same families were forced to remove and abandon their land (and any improvements) in June of 1838 and told they would receive payment. Peck laments that no compensation has yet been received by those families. He also discusses several land claims and attempts to receive payment. Digital image of original manuscript, scanned by the University of Georgia Libraries in 2000, as part of GALILEO, funded in part by a grant from the Institute of Museum and Library Services.

data.theme: "Trail of Tears" OR "Indian Removal Act"
data.title: B.B. Cannon Journal
data.description: In 1837, B.B. Cannon oversaw a group of Cherokees who were forced from their land in Tennessee by the United States government. The group started out in Tennessee and passed through parts of Kentucky, Illinois, Missouri, Arkansas and finally reached their destination in Oklahoma. There were about 365 people at the start of the journey, and fifteen Native Americans died along the way. The journey took 77 days.

data.theme: "Navajo Indians" OR "Navajo Nation" OR "Diné" OR "Dineh" OR "Navajo people" OR "Navajo reservation"
data.title: No. 49: Mariano Martinez, Chief of the Navajo Indians
data.description: A black and white wash drawing on medium-weight paper by Edward Meyer Kern based on a sketch by his brother, Richard H. Kern, depicting Mariano Martinez, one of two Navajo chiefs to be signatories of an 1849 treaty with Colonel Washington of the US Army expedition. This side portrait shows Martinez in a greatcoat, head wrap, and narrow-brimmed hat. Cataloging made possible by The Getty Grant Program.

value.text: National Alliance to Combat Racism and Political Oppression in America
Legal Offense-Defense Committee of Wounded Knee.
National Alliance to Combat Racism and Political Oppression in America
Legal Offense-Defense Committee of Wounded Knee.

data.theme: "Red Power movement" OR "Red Power" OR "American Indian Movement"
data.title: American Indian Movement Symposium; KUOM Lecture Series
data.description: This symposium was co-sponsored by the American Indian Movement, the Department of American Indian Studies at the University of Minnesota, and the Wounded Knee Legal Offense-Defense Committee. The essence of each speaker's remarks is presented in this selection. The speakers include: Indian leader Russell Means, Vernon Bellecourt, Clyde Bellecourt, and Eddie Benton.; Dr. Roger Buffalohead from the University of Minnesota; Angela Davis, Chairperson of the National Alliance to Combat Racism and Political Oppression in America, and attorney Mark Lane, member of the Legal Offense-Defense Committee of Wounded Knee. The symposium offered interested persons a chance to come together to learn the facts about Wounded Knee, discuss the issues, and offer mutual encouragement and support. The AIM anthem is played. Speakers revieew the history and purpose of AIM, which was founded in Minneapolis in July 1968. Vernon Bellecourt offers an overview of the oppression of Indians in U.S. History.Mark Lane reviews the entire Wounded Knee Incident in detail. He strongly defends the actions of AIM leaders and bitterly criticizes the actions of the government and the news media. Speakers stress the fact that the American Indian Movement has become a spiritual movement as well as a political force.

data.theme: "Trail of Tears" OR "Indian Removal Act"
data.title: Cherokee Legends and the Trail of Tears
data.description: A series of Cherokee myths and legends. Also includes the John Burnett version of the Cherokee Removal better known as the Trail of Tears. Chapters include: 1. How the Earth Was Made 2. The Rattlesnake's Vengeance 3. How the Milky Way Came to Be 4. Why the Possum's Tail is Bare 5. Ataga'hi, the Magic Lake 6. The Race Between the Crane and the Hummingbird 7. Why the Buzzard's Head is Bare 8. Why the Mink Smells 9. Cherokee Indian Ball Game 10. Removal of the Cherokees 1838-39 11. They Katydid's Warning 12. The First Fire
